common_name: Zebra Calathea (Zebra Plant, Zebra Prayer Plant)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded container plant in zone 9; Zebra Calathea cannot tolerate temperatures below 60°F or drafts from air conditioning and heating vents. It requires high humidity (50–70%) for best appearance — leaves will curl and develop brown edges in dry air. Use a pebble tray with water or a humidifier nearby. Water with filtered, distilled, or rainwater (tap water fluoride causes leaf tip burn). Propagate by rhizome division in spring.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Zebra Calathea performs best with consistent bright indirect light (avoid direct sun which bleaches the markings) and will tolerate lower light better than most ornamental tropicals, though growth slows considerably. Calathea zebrina is a large, bold tropical from the humid forests of southeastern Brazil, prized for the extraordinary pattern on its large, elongated, velvety leaves: vivid alternating stripes of dark emerald-green and pale lime-green radiate from the central midrib in precise, regular bands like the markings of a zebra, while the undersides of the leaves are deep burgundy-purple. The velvety, matte leaf surface (from microscopic papillae) absorbs light rather than reflecting it, creating an unusually rich, deep color. As a prayer plant in the Marantaceae family, the leaves fold upward at night and open horizontally during the day — when a large clump of Zebra Calathea opens in morning light, the movement is visible. This is one of the largest and most dramatically marked of the popular Calatheas, making it an excellent statement foliage plant for shaded indoor spaces and sheltered outdoor patios in warm climates. Non-toxic to pets and children.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Calathea zebrina cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calathea species for indoor use
Tropical Plant Society — Marantaceae: prayer plant family
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Calathea zebrina (non-toxic)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded patio container in zone 9; Rattlesnake Plant requires consistent warmth (65–85°F), moderate indirect light, and high humidity (50%+) for best appearance. Water with filtered or rainwater to avoid fluoride damage to leaf tips. The narrow, upright leaves make it one of the more efficient space users in the Calathea group. Propagate by rhizome division in spring when repotting. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade in moist, well-drained, well-aerated potting mix; Rattlesnake Plant is one of the most adaptable Calatheas for indoor conditions, tolerating somewhat lower humidity and slightly less consistent care than many of its relatives while maintaining attractive foliage. Calathea lancifolia (sometimes reclassified as Goeppertia insignis) is a prayer plant from the tropical rainforests of Brazil, producing distinctively narrow, elongated lance-shaped leaves up to 18 inches long decorated with a precise, alternating pattern of dark oval spots along the midrib that graduates to wavy, irregular dark green edges — a marking pattern that strikingly resembles the scale pattern of certain rattlesnakes, particularly the timber rattlesnake, hence the common name. The undersides of the leaves are deep purple-red, creating a dramatic visual effect when the leaves move through their daily opening and closing cycle (nyctinasty). Among the popular Calatheas, Rattlesnake Plant is often recommended as the best choice for beginners due to its relatively higher tolerance for imperfect conditions — it is more forgiving of occasional dry periods, lower humidity, and temperature fluctuation than species like C. ornata or C. makoyana, while still producing the characteristic dramatic patterned foliage that makes the group so appealing.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Calathea lancifolia cultivation
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Goeppertia (Calathea) species care
American Horticulture Society — Prayer plants: Marantaceae family care
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Calathea lancifolia (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Red Tower Ginger (Scarlet Spiral Ginger, Costus barbatus)

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or container-grown clumps in spring when soil temperature reaches 65°F+ in partial shade with consistently moist, humus-rich soil; Red Tower Ginger grows best in the dappled light of woodland gardens and shaded tropical beds. In zone 9, the rhizomes survive mild winters underground; mulch heavily in zone 9a to insulate roots from hard frost. The striking red cone-shaped terminal inflorescences begin forming in summer. Divide clumps every 3–4 years. The spirally arranged leaves on the canes are the characteristic feature of the entire Costus genus.

directions: Plant in partial shade to dappled light in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Red Tower Ginger thrives in the conditions of its native Costa Rican and Colombian rainforest habitat — warm, humid, partially shaded, with consistently moist soil. Costus barbatus is a dramatic ornamental ginger from Central and South America (Costa Rica, Colombia) in the Costaceae family, distinguished by its tall, cane-like stems whose leaves are arranged in a distinctive spiral pattern rather than the two-ranked arrangement of true gingers (Zingiber) — giving the genus its common name 'Spiral Ginger.' The primary ornamental feature is the inflorescence: a dense, cylindrical to cone-shaped terminal structure of overlapping, hard, bright scarlet to orange-red bracts from which small yellow flowers emerge — like a red torch or pinecone of living fire at the top of each cane. The inflorescences persist for weeks and are occasionally used as cut flowers or in tropical arrangements. Red Tower Ginger is one of the most dramatic ornamental gingers for zone 9 Gulf Coast shade gardens where its bold, architectural form — spirally-leaved canes topped with scarlet torches — creates a powerfully tropical effect in woodland and shaded tropical beds.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Costus species for Florida shade gardens
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Costus barbatus ornamental ginger notes
LSU AgCenter — Ornamental Gingers for Shaded Gulf Coast Gardens
International Society for Horticultural Science — Costaceae: ornamental gingers

common_name: Iron Cross Begonia (Iron Cross Plant, Beefsteak Geranium)

planting.note: Grow as a houseplant or shaded container plant in zone 9; Iron Cross Begonia cannot tolerate frost or temperatures below 55°F. It is rhizomatous — grow in a wide, shallow container to allow horizontal rhizome spread. Keep the rhizome at soil level (do not bury it) and allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings. High humidity (50–70%) improves appearance and prevents powdery mildew. Propagate from leaf cuttings or rhizome sections. Excellent for terrariums.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to moderate shade — Iron Cross Begonia thrives in the bright, indirect light of a north or east window and will scorch in direct sun. Begonia masoniana is a rhizomatous begonia from China (Guangxi province) and South Vietnam with one of the most immediately recognizable leaf patterns in the houseplant world: the large, deeply textured, quilted, bright-green, heart-shaped leaves bear a bold, dark brown to blackish pattern at the center that precisely resembles a German Iron Cross or a four-pointed star within a circle — a marking that appears on every leaf with striking consistency and regularity. The textured, bumpy leaf surface — covered in fine hairs and raised blisters — adds a three-dimensional quality to the ornamental effect. Small, white to pale pink flowers appear in spring but are secondary to the dramatic foliage. Iron Cross Begonia prefers the warm, humid conditions of its subtropical native habitat and will maintain beautiful foliage year-round in appropriate indoor conditions. It is more tolerant of lower humidity than cane-type begonias but still benefits from supplemental humidity in dry indoor environments. The leaf pattern is so distinctive and consistent that it was originally thought to be a deliberate human selection, but it occurs naturally in the wild population.
sources: Royal Horticultural Society — Begonia masoniana cultivation
American Begonia Society — Rhizomatous Begonias: Begonia masoniana
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Begonia species for indoor use
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Begonia masoniana

common_name: Victoria Water Lily (Royal Water Platter, Queen Victoria Water Lily)

planting.note: Start from seed indoors in warm water (80–90°F) in late winter and transplant to an outdoor pond or large water feature after last frost when water temperature reaches 75°F+; Victoria amazonica grows extremely rapidly once established in warm water. It requires a large water feature (minimum 20×20 feet, 3–4 feet deep) to reach its full glory. In zone 9, grow as a warm-season annual — plants cannot survive cold water below 60°F. Botanical gardens and public water gardens throughout the Gulf South grow Victoria species annually as statement plants.

directions: Plant in full sun in large, warm, fertile ponds or water features — Victoria amazonica requires the warmest, sunniest conditions and a large volume of nutrient-rich water for its enormous, rapid growth. Victoria amazonica is the world's largest water lily, producing the most spectacular leaves of any aquatic plant: circular, flat, floating pads up to 10 feet across (in exceptional specimens) with upturned margins 4–8 inches high that give them the appearance of enormous green trays or pizza-pan platters on the water surface. The underside of each pad is covered with sharp spines (to deter fish feeding) and a matrix of rigid, air-filled ribs of extraordinary structural efficiency — the geometry of these ribs, allowing the leaf to support a weight of 100+ pounds distributed evenly across its surface, inspired Victorian engineer Joseph Paxton's design for the Crystal Palace in London (1851). The flowers are large (12–18 inches), open white at night and release an intense pineapple-vanilla fragrance to attract scarab beetles as pollinators, then close at dawn, trap the beetles, release pollen, and reopen the following night — now brilliant pink to rose-red. In zone 9, Victoria amazonica is grown as an annual summer spectacle in public water gardens and large private ponds.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Victoria amazonica species profile
Missouri Botanical Garden — Victoria amazonica cultivation notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aquatic Ornamentals: Victoria species
Paxton, J. (1851) Crystal Palace structural notes — Victoria leaf rib geometry
